name: Vasugupta
dates_notes: Founder of the Spanda lineage; teacher of Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa; dated by guru-paramparā with Kallaṭa and by the traditional account of the revelation of the Śiva-Sūtra at Mahādeva mountain.
core_thesis: Vasugupta is the figure to whom the *Śiva-Sūtra* — the founding revealed scripture of the Trika tradition after the Tantras themselves — is traditionally credited, on the account that Śiva revealed the seventy-seven sūtras to him on the rock of Mahādeva mountain in Kashmir. The sūtras are organized into three *unmeṣas* — *Śāmbhava* (*ŚS* 1.1–22), *Śākta* (*ŚS* 2.1–10), *Āṇava* (*ŚS* 3.1–45) — corresponding to three soteriological registers, and open with *caitanyam ātmā* (*ŚS* 1.1, "the self is consciousness itself"). The Spanda doctrine — that consciousness is intrinsically a pulsation (*spanda*) by which the entire manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed ([*Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1](cite://vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1), *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau*) — is on one tradition Vasugupta's own articulation in the *Spanda-Kārikā*; on another the *Kārikā* is the work of his disciple Kallaṭa. *Spanda* in either case names the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole*: closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the totality-scope is established by *SpK* 1.1 *jagataḥ* covering the entire cosmic field). Vasugupta thus founds the second of the three streams (Spanda, Pratyabhijñā, Kula / Krama) Abhinavagupta later draws on in the *Tantrāloka* ([*Tantrāloka* 1.5](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/1.5) acknowledging the Spanda inheritance).

engaged_works.title: Shiva-Sutra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Vasugupta as the recipient of Śiva's revelation; the school's foundational scripture after the Tantras themselves. The traditional account is that Śiva revealed the sūtras to Vasugupta on the rock of Mahādeva mountain.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal acceptance within the Trika tradition; the ascription is constitutive of the school's self-understanding.
Kṣemarāja's Śiva-Sūtra-Vimarśinī presupposes Vasugupta's role as recipient.

engaged_works.summary: The seventy-seven sūtras of the Trika tradition's foundational scripture, organized into three *unmeṣas* corresponding to the three *upāyas* of progressively descending subtlety. The *Śāmbhava-unmeṣa* (*ŚS* 1.1–1.22) opens with *caitanyam ātmā* (*ŚS* 1.1, "the self is consciousness itself") and unfolds the immediate Śāmbhava path of recognition without method: the supreme self is identified (*ŚS* 1.1), the bonds are identified as the contents of awareness (*ŚS* 1.2 *jñānaṃ bandhaḥ* — note the inversion of the Vedāntic frame: it is *knowledge as content* that binds, not ignorance as such), and liberation is identified as the immediate recognition of one's own consciousness as Śiva (*ŚS* 1.4–7, the *jñāna-prakāśa* sequence). The *Śākta-unmeṣa* (*ŚS* 2.1–2.10) gives the path of attentive *vimarśa*: the practitioner works through cognition's rising-and-subsiding rhythm to discern the *spanda* that underlies it (*ŚS* 2.1 *cittaṃ mantraḥ*; *ŚS* 2.7 *mātṛkā-cakra-saṃbodhaḥ*). The *Āṇava-unmeṣa* (*ŚS* 3.1–3.45) gives the path of breath, mantra, and meditative supports for those still bound to the limited subject, including the famous sūtras on the *kuṇḍalinī*-pulse (*ŚS* 3.5 *nāḍī-saṃhāra*) and on the structure of yogic awakening (*ŚS* 3.27–32). The text is the canonical scripture on which the Spanda-Kārikā, Kṣemarāja's *Vimarśinī*, and the entire Trika living tradition are built.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Authorship disputed within the school's own tradition: one tradition (followed by Kṣemarāja in the Spanda-Nirṇaya) credits the Kārikā to Vasugupta as a verse-distillation of the Spanda doctrine implicit in the Śiva-Sūtra; the other tradition credits the Kārikā to Vasugupta's disciple Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa. The text itself is anonymous in its earliest manuscript witnesses. Modern scholarship is divided; Mark Dyczkowski's studies are the principal modern treatment of the question.

ascription_evidence.description: Two competing traditions of ascription, both with internal Trika support; the text's authorship cannot be settled on present evidence.
Both ascriptions are accepted as legitimate within different sub-strands of the Trika tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The fifty-three verses of the foundational text of the *Spanda* tradition, organized into four *niṣyandas* (sections). The first ([*Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1–25](cite://vasugupta/spanda-karika/1.1-25)) establishes the doctrine of *spanda*: consciousness is intrinsically a pulsation (*spanda*) by which the entire manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed (*SpK* 1.1, *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau*). *Spanda* is the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole* — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the totality-scope is established by the *jagataḥ* of *SpK* 1.1 covering the entire cosmic field). The second *niṣyanda* (*SpK* 2.1–7) analyzes the operation of *spanda* in the bound subject: how the *spanda*-pulse appears as the rising and subsiding of cognitions, emotions, and the entire experiential field, and how attention to that pulse is the entry into recognition. The third *niṣyanda* (*SpK* 3.1–19) gives the practical *upāyas* by which the practitioner aligns awareness with the *spanda*. The fourth (*SpK* 4.1–2) gives the condition of full recognition, in which the practitioner's awareness and Śiva's *spanda* are identical. Authorship of the text is contested between Vasugupta and Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa within the school's own tradition; the entry honors the dispute by listing the work under both authors with full ascription notes.

reception_notes: Vasugupta is the founding figure of the Spanda tradition and the recipient of the Śiva-Sūtra revelation that grounds the entire Trika lineage as a self-aware school. Kṣemarāja's Śiva-Sūtra-Vimarśinī is the standard commentary; the Spanda-Kārikā is read together with the Spanda-Nirṇaya and the Spanda-Sandoha. Modern scholarly recovery — Dyczkowski's studies on Spanda, Singh's translations of the Śiva-Sūtra, ongoing work in the Pondicherry School — has restored Vasugupta to his foundational place within the Trika genealogy.
